summary: DHH celebrates the addition of drag-and-drop reordering to Backpack, enabled by the script.aculo.us and Prototype JavaScript libraries. He argues this represents disruptive technology eating its way up the capability chain, narrowing the gap between web and desktop applications. The key breakthrough isn't the feature itself but how easy it is to implement, thanks to libraries that abstract away complexity. He previews that Rails will bundle these tools, pushing web application development further toward rich interactivity without requiring deep JavaScript expertise.
key_points: Disruptive technologies work by gradually climbing the capability chain, and Ajax-powered DHTML is doing exactly that for web applications
The real significance of drag-and-drop in Backpack isn't the feature itself but that libraries like script.aculo.us make it trivially easy to implement
Accessibility and sharing of web content already outweighed the richer experience of desktop apps; closing the UX gap tips the scales further
Rails will bundle these JavaScript libraries directly, making rich web interactions a default rather than a specialized effort
money_quotes: One of the hallmarks of disruptive technologies is that they eat their way up through the chain of capabilities over time.
The gab to mimicking the rich desktop applications is growing ever more narrow.
The real reason this is a big deal is because the rocket science department has been put on leave.
By taking the complexity out of the ordeal, it becomes more accessible, less of a project, and more something you just do.
Make it as easy as not to.
Rails is going to push the frontier of integration once again. Everything needed to build web applications like Backpack.
key_insight: The true power of new web technologies isn't what they enable but how they lower the barrier so that advanced features become something you just do rather than a specialized project.
